is a person playing in one of the three defensive positions in [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") or [softball](/wiki/Softball "Softball"), farthest from the batter. These defenders are the [left fielder](/wiki/Left_fielder "Left fielder"),[[2]](#cite_note-left-field_mlb_glossary-2) the [center fielder](/wiki/Center_fielder "Center fielder"),[[3]](#cite_note-center-fielder_mlb_glossary-3) and the [right fielder](/wiki/Right_fielder "Right fielder").[[4]](#cite_note-right-fielder_mlb_glossary-4) As an outfielder, their duty is to catch [fly balls](/wiki/Batted_ball "Batted ball") and ground balls then to return them to the infield for the out or before the runner advances, if there are any runners on the [bases](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball"). Outfielders normally play behind the six [defensive players](/wiki/Baseball_positions "Baseball positions") located in the [infield](/wiki/Infield "Infield"): the [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher"), [catcher](/wiki/Catcher "Catcher"), [first baseman](/wiki/First_baseman "First baseman"), [second baseman](/wiki/Second_baseman "Second baseman"), [third baseman](/wiki/Third_baseman "Third baseman"), and [shortstop](/wiki/Shortstop "Shortstop"). The left fielder and right fielder are named based on their positions relative to the center fielder when looking out from [home plate](/wiki/Home_plate "Home plate"), with the left fielder positioned to the left of the center fielder and the right fielder positioned to the right.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Defensive position in baseball

This article is about the baseball and softball position. For the association football (soccer) term, see [outfield player](/wiki/Outfield_player "Outfield player"). For the cricket position, see [Fielding (cricket)](/wiki/Fielding_(cricket) "Fielding (cricket)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Ty_Cobb_Paul_Thompson%2C_c1918.jpg/250px-Ty_Cobb_Paul_Thompson%2C_c1918.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ty_Cobb_Paul_Thompson,_c1918.jpg)

[Ty Cobb](/wiki/Ty_Cobb "Ty Cobb") holds the record for most games played as an outfielder in [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") history, with 2,934.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

An **outfielder** is a person playing in one of the three defensive positions in [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") or [softball](/wiki/Softball "Softball"), farthest from the batter. These defenders are the [left fielder](/wiki/Left_fielder "Left fielder"),[[2]](#cite_note-left-field_mlb_glossary-2) the [center fielder](/wiki/Center_fielder "Center fielder"),[[3]](#cite_note-center-fielder_mlb_glossary-3) and the [right fielder](/wiki/Right_fielder "Right fielder").[[4]](#cite_note-right-fielder_mlb_glossary-4) As an outfielder, their duty is to catch [fly balls](/wiki/Batted_ball "Batted ball") and ground balls then to return them to the infield for the out or before the runner advances, if there are any runners on the [bases](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball"). Outfielders normally play behind the six [defensive players](/wiki/Baseball_positions "Baseball positions") located in the [infield](/wiki/Infield "Infield"): the [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher"), [catcher](/wiki/Catcher "Catcher"), [first baseman](/wiki/First_baseman "First baseman"), [second baseman](/wiki/Second_baseman "Second baseman"), [third baseman](/wiki/Third_baseman "Third baseman"), and [shortstop](/wiki/Shortstop "Shortstop"). The left fielder and right fielder are named based on their positions relative to the center fielder when looking out from [home plate](/wiki/Home_plate "Home plate"), with the left fielder positioned to the left of the center fielder and the right fielder positioned to the right.

By convention, each of the nine defensive positions in baseball are numbered. The outfield positions are 7 (left fielder), 8 (center fielder) and 9 (right fielder). These numbers are shorthand designations useful in [baseball scorekeeping](/wiki/Baseball_scorekeeping "Baseball scorekeeping") and are not the same as the [squad numbers](/wiki/Uniform_number "Uniform number") worn on player uniforms.

Outfielders named to the [MLB All-Century Team](/wiki/MLB_All-Century_Team "MLB All-Century Team") are [Hank Aaron](/wiki/Hank_Aaron "Hank Aaron"), [Ty Cobb](/wiki/Ty_Cobb "Ty Cobb"), [Joe DiMaggio](/wiki/Joe_DiMaggio "Joe DiMaggio"), [Mickey Mantle](/wiki/Mickey_Mantle "Mickey Mantle"), [Willie Mays](/wiki/Willie_Mays "Willie Mays"), [Stan Musial](/wiki/Stan_Musial "Stan Musial"), [Pete Rose](/wiki/Pete_Rose "Pete Rose"), [Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth"), [Ted Williams](/wiki/Ted_Williams "Ted Williams"), [Carl Yastrzemski](/wiki/Carl_Yastrzemski "Carl Yastrzemski"), and [Ken Griffey Jr.](/wiki/Ken_Griffey_Jr. "Ken Griffey Jr.")[[5]](#cite_note-all-century_team-5)

## Strategy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Outfielder&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Strategy")]

Players can be characterized as either [corner outfielders](/wiki/Corner_outfielders "Corner outfielders") or a [center fielder](/wiki/Center_fielder "Center fielder"). Corner outfielders are often slower and have less defensive value than the [center fielder](/wiki/Center_fielder "Center fielder"). However, there are some important differences between [right fielders](/wiki/Right_fielder "Right fielder") and [left fielders](/wiki/Left_fielder "Left fielder"). Right fielders tend to have the best throwing arms of the outfield so they can make the long throw to third base, but often are not as fast as left fielders. Center fielders are generally the fastest and most athletic of the three, because they have to run the farthest in order to field balls in the gaps and back up the other outfielders when balls are hit to them. Outfielders should also be able to read where the ball may be placed based on what the pitcher is throwing. They can tell what the pitcher is throwing by the middle infielders, second base and short stop, in which they show the numbers the catcher is giving to the pitcher behind their back to determine the pitch and tell where the ball could possibly be hit to.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Many of the best power hitters in baseball play in the [outfield](/wiki/Outfield "Outfield"), where they do not have as constant involvement in fielding plays as other positions, especially before the institution of the [designated hitter](/wiki/Designated_hitter "Designated hitter"). For example, [Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth") was moved from [pitcher](/wiki/Pitcher "Pitcher") to the outfield.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Left fielders and right fielders are more often slow power hitters, and center fielders are usually fast base-runners and good defensive players. Center field is often considered the most difficult outfield position, requiring both a good throwing arm and speed. Center fielders on many teams often bat [lead off](/wiki/Leadoff_hitter "Leadoff hitter").

Players who do not routinely start games, but often substitute as a pinch hitter or defensive replacement in the outfield are referred to as [fourth outfielders](/wiki/Fourth_outfielder "Fourth outfielder") or even [fifth outfielders](/wiki/Fifth_outfielder "Fifth outfielder"). These players can usually play any of the three outfield positions.

## Corner outfielders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Outfielder&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Corner outfielders")]

Corner outfielders are outfielders who play the corner positions of [right field](/wiki/Right_field "Right field") and [left field](/wiki/Left_field "Left field"). Corner outfielders often have less speed than [center fielders](/wiki/Center_fielder "Center fielder"), but make up for this deficiency with their offensive play.

Left fielders usually handle more chances because right-handed hitters tend to hit balls to the left, and right-[handedness](/wiki/Handedness "Handedness") is more common in baseball, as in most things, than left-handedness. Many left fielders have the speed to play center field, but lack the throwing ability required.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Right fielders typically have stronger arms, but are frequently (not always) slower and less agile defensively. Right fielders typically need stronger arms than left fielders because the throw from right field to each base (except first base) is longer than the throw from left field to each base; also, because [baserunners](/wiki/Baserunner "Baserunner") run counterclockwise around the bases, they're usually running away from the right fielder. Many corner outfielders are capable of playing in either right or left field.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

An example of an ultra-fast left fielder is [Rickey Henderson](/wiki/Rickey_Henderson "Rickey Henderson") ([Ben Oglivie](/wiki/Ben_Oglivie "Ben Oglivie") and [Lou Brock](/wiki/Lou_Brock "Lou Brock") can fit this description too), whereas the slow-footed but very strong-armed [Carl Furillo](/wiki/Carl_Furillo "Carl Furillo"), "The Reading Rifle", sets a standard for right fielders in the terms specified here.
